name: Hausa-Fulani
description: The Hausa are a Sahel and savannah people whose historic city-states (Kano, Katsina, Zaria, Sokoto) formed major trade and Islamic scholarship centres from the 12th century. The Fulani (Fulbe) are a wide-ranging pastoralist people who swept across West Africa in a series of 19th-century Islamic jihads, intermarrying with the Hausa to form the Hausa-Fulani political identity. Together they dominate northern Nigeria and are the largest Muslim cultural bloc in Africa south of the Sahara.
cultural_highlights: Durbar festival — a magnificent display of mounted cavalry in royal regalia, performed for Eid and state visits; one of Africa's most spectacular royal ceremonies
Islamic scholarship — Kano and Katsina were centres of trans-Saharan learning; Usman dan Fodio's 19th-century Caliphate unified the region
Hausa architecture — tall mud-brick buildings with decorated interiors using geometric arabesques; the Kano Old City is a UNESCO site
Talakawa social philosophy — the value of communal dignity; the Hausa social contract where nobility carries responsibility to subjects
Adire (indigo cloth) — intricate resist-dyed indigo textiles traded across the Sahara

food_traditions.name: Tuwo Shinkafa
food_traditions.description: Rice pudding-like stew eaten with miyan taushe (pumpkin soup) — the flagship northern Nigerian meal

food_traditions.name: Suya
food_traditions.description: Spiced grilled meat on skewers rubbed with groundnut cake (kuli-kuli) — the most famous Nigerian street food globally

food_traditions.name: Kunu
food_traditions.description: Fermented grain drink made from sorghum or millet — a refreshing daily beverage served in calabashes

food_traditions.name: Masa (rice cakes)
food_traditions.description: Fermented rice cakes cooked in a special mould; a northern breakfast staple eaten with honey or soup

music_traditions: Kakaki trumpet — a 3-metre royal trumpet announcing the Emir's presence, one of the longest traditional instruments in Africa
Goge fiddle — a one-string spike fiddle used in praise music for nobles and at ceremonies
Waka music — women's sung poetry combining Islamic influences with Hausa oral traditions
